text: **The Book of Strange Things** *By Cao Pi of the Three Kingdoms Period* Emperor Huangdi was buried in Qiaoshan, but when the mountain collapsed, his body was nowhere to be found, leaving only his sword and shoes behind. *Juyan* 697. During the reign of Duke Mu of Qin, a man from Chen Cang dug up an unusual object; its shape did not resemble a dog or a sheep, and no one could name it. Some versions of *Juyan* suggest it looked like neither a sheep nor a pig. The man brought it to Duke Mu of Qin, and along the way, he encountered two children. The children said, "This is called an 'ao,' which *Juyan* refers to as 'yuan.' The note in *Shuowen Jiezi* explains that 'yuan' is pronounced 'ao' and lives underground, feeding on the brains of the dead. If you want to kill it, insert a pine branch into its head." This account is also found in *Juyan* 375 and 954. The 'ao' then said, "Those two children are named Chen Bao; whoever captures the male will become a king, and whoever captures the female will become a hegemon." The man from Chen Cang abandoned the 'ao' and chased the two children, who transformed into pheasants and flew into a flat forest. He reported this to Duke Mu of Qin, who then organized a large hunt and indeed captured the female. It transformed into a stone and was placed between the rivers Qian and Wei. By the time of Duke Wen of Qin, a shrine was built for it, named Chen Bao. The male flew south and settled in what is now the county of Zhi in Nanyang. This is also recorded in *Guangji* 461. The Qin state wanted to commemorate this omen, hence naming the county after it. Every time the Chen Cang shrine is celebrated, a red light over ten zhang long appears, coming from Zhi County and entering the Chen Bao shrine, accompanied by a sound like a rooster. This is also noted in *Leiju* 90, *Juyan* 917, and *Shuchao* 89.

The Index of the "Records of the Grand Historian" chapter on Sacrifices to Heaven and Earth cites the *Book of Strange Phenomena* (列異傳) as follows: A person from Chen Cang found a strange object and presented it to someone. On the way, they met two children who said, "This is called媦, and it feeds on the brains of the dead below." The spirit then said, "Those two children are named Chen Bao; whoever captures the male will become a king, and whoever captures the female will become a hegemon." They chased the children, who then transformed into pheasants. Duke Mu of Qin conducted a great hunt and indeed captured the female pheasant, so he built a shrine for it. During the worship, there were flashes of light, thunder, and lightning. The male pheasant settled in Nanyang, and a red light several tens of feet long came into the Chen Cang shrine. In the ancient county of Gudao in Wudu, there is a shrine to a fierce bull, which is said to have originally been a great mulberry tree in the south mountain. In the 27th year of Duke Wen of Qin's reign, they attacked it, and the wound on the tree healed immediately. Duke Wen of Qin then sent forty men with axes to chop it down, but it did not break. One tired soldier, who injured his foot and could not leave, lay under the tree. He heard the ghosts talking to each other: "Are you tired of attacking?" One said, "We are very tired." Another said, "Duke Wen of Qin will surely keep attacking." The other replied, "What can he do to me?" The first one said, "What about covering it with red ashes?" Then they remained silent. The soldier reported this, and Duke Wen of Qin ordered the soldiers to wear red clothes, follow where they cut, and cover the tree with ashes. It broke off and transformed into a cow, entering the water. So Qin built a shrine for it. Commentary on the Wei River chapter of the *Waterways Classic* (水經渭水篇注).

During the reign of Duke Wen of Qin, a paulownia tree transformed into a cow. A rider attacked it, but the horse could not defeat it. The rider fell to the ground, and his hair came loose and fell out. The cow was frightened and entered the water. Therefore, the Qin state established horsemen with feathered headdresses and sent them as vanguard. According to the *Hou Hanshu* (Book of the Later Han) and other sources, the cow was so frightened that it entered the waters of the Feng River, and the Qin state subsequently built a temple called the "Temple of the Angry Ox." Gan Jiang and Mo Ye were swordsmiths who forged swords for the king of Chu. After three years, they completed the swords, which were renowned for their exceptional quality. The swords were divided into male and female, and the male sword was kept by Mo Ye while the female sword was given to the king. Mo Ye told his wife, "I have hidden the male sword in the north side of the South Mountain, on the south side of the North Mountain. It is embedded in a pine tree growing on a stone. If the king discovers it and kills me, if you give birth to a son, tell him." When the king eventually discovered the sword and killed Mo Ye, his wife later gave birth to a son named Chibei, who was told about the sword. Chibei chopped down a pine tree in the South Mountain but did not find the sword. He suddenly found it inside a house pillar. The king of Chu dreamed of a man with a three-inch-wide forehead who said he wanted to take revenge. The search was very urgent, and the man escaped to the Zhuxing Mountain. He met a guest who wanted to help him take revenge. The man cut off his head and offered it to the king. The guest ordered the head to be cooked in a large pot, but it did not rot for three days and three nights. The king went to see it, and the guest leaned and struck the king with the male sword, causing the king's head to fall into the pot. The guest then took his own life. The three heads were all cooked and could not be distinguished, so they were buried separately and named the "Tomb of the Three Kings."

### Translation Wei Wuji, the Prince of Wei, was once reading in his room when a dove flew into his desk, and a hawk chased and killed it. Wei Wuji was furious at the hawk's attack and ordered the capture of hawks throughout the country, resulting in the capture of over two hundred. He pressed his sword and said to the hawks in the cage, "Those who grasped the dove should lower their heads and confess, and those who did not should flap their wings." One hawk remained motionless. *Guangji* 460. Lu Shaoqian obtained a talisman from a celestial being. The youngest daughter of the King of Chu was afflicted by a demon and requested Lu Shaoqian's help. Lu Shaoqian stopped to rest a few miles away from the palace. At night, a carriage covered with a turtle shell and accompanied by thousands of horsemen arrived. The driver introduced himself as Bo Jing and awaited Lu Shaoqian. He invited Lu Shaoqian to drink several cups of wine and enjoy several tables of food. Before parting, Bo Jing said, "The illness of the King of Chu's daughter was caused by me. If you can help her, I will repay you with twenty million coins." Lu Shaoqian accepted the money and returned to the palace by another route. He treated the princess, and before her room, someone pushed the door open and said, "Shaoqian deceived your father!" A strong wind then blew toward the northwest, and when they looked, there was blood in a basin. The princess then lost consciousness but revived at midnight. The king sent someone to follow the wind and found a dead snake several meters long with thousands of smaller snakes lying dead beside it in the northwest of the city. Later, the imperial edict ordered the counties to investigate the incident, and it was found that the Grand Farmer had lost twenty million coins, and the Imperial Kitchen had lost several tables of food. Lu Shaoqian submitted a report with all the details, and the emperor was amazed. *Guangji* 456.

During the reign of Emperor Wen (甘露 period), Gong Sun Da served as the magistrate of Chen County and died in office. As the preparations for his burial were underway, his children and dozens of local officials attended the funeral. Gong Sun Da had a five-year-old son who suddenly began speaking as if possessed by a spirit, his voice sounding like his father's. He scolded the crowd: "The mourning cry has already been mentioned in the first twelve characters of the *Yuelu* (a collection of notes and anecdotes). I want to say something." He then called his children and taught them in turn. The children were overwhelmed with grief and unable to bear it, so he comforted them by saying, "The cycle of the four seasons still has an end; human life is short and fragile, but there is no limit." He spoke thousands of words like this, all of which formed coherent statements. The child then asked, "When people die, they are all unaware. Only you, my father, are exceptionally wise and unique, so do you alone have a spirit?" He replied, "The matter of life and death is not easily explained; the affairs of ghosts and spirits are beyond human knowledge." He asked for paper to write and filled it with meaningful words. He threw the paper on the ground and said, "Seal this letter and send it to the Lord of Wei. If there is a letter from him, deliver it to him." Indeed, a letter came from the Lord of Wei. (This story is recorded in *Yuelu* 884 and *Guangji* 316.)

汉中有鬼神栾侯，常在承尘上喜食鲊菜，书钞一百四十六引云汉川神常在承尘上喜食鲊菜能知吉凶。甘露中，大蝗起：所经处禾稼辄尽。太守遣使告栾侯，祀以鲊菜。侯谓吏曰：“蝗虫小事，辄当除之。”。言讫，翕然飞出。吏仿彿其状类鸠，声如水鸟。吏还，具白太守？果有众鸟亿万，来食蝗虫，须臾皆尽。广记二百九十二 In Hanzhong, there is a ghost deity named "栾侯," who often resides on the ceiling and enjoys eating fermented fish and vegetables. According to volume 146 of the *Shu Chao* (Book of Selections), the deity of Hanzhong, residing on the ceiling, enjoys eating fermented fish and vegetables and can predict good or bad omens. During the "甘露" era, a severe locust infestation occurred, and wherever the locusts passed, the crops were completely destroyed. The governor sent a messenger to inform "栾侯" and offered him fermented fish and vegetables as an offering. "栾侯" told the officials, "The locusts are a minor issue; they should be eliminated." After speaking, the locusts suddenly flew away. The officials vaguely described the shape as similar to a dove and the sound as like that of a waterbird. The officials returned and reported to the governor, and indeed, millions of birds came to eat the locusts, consuming them all in a short time. *Guangji* (Broad Records) 292. 西河鲜于冀，建武中为清河太守，言出钱六百万作屋，未成而死。赵高代之，计功用钱，凡二百万耳。五官黄秉功曹刘商言是冀所自取，便表没冀田宅奴婢，妻子送日南。俄而白日冀鬼入府，与商秉等共计较，定余钱二百万，皆商等匿。冀乃表自列付商上，诏还冀田宅。御览八百三十六 In the "建武" era, Xianyu Ji of Xihhe served as the governor of Qinghe. He claimed to have spent six million coins building a house, but he died before completing it. Zhao Gao succeeded him, and after calculating the expenses, it turned out to be only two million coins. The officers Huang Bing and Liu Shang said it was taken by Ji himself, and they then reported to the emperor to confiscate Ji's property, slaves, and send his wife and children to Rizhong. Soon after, the ghost of Ji entered the government office and argued with Huang Bing and Liu Shang, settling on a remaining amount of two million coins, which they had concealed. Ji then reported this to the emperor, who ordered the return of his property. *Yulian* (Imperial Review) 836. 寿光侯者，汉章帝时人，劾百鬼众魅。有妇为魅所疾，侯劾得大蛇；又有大树，人止之者死，侯劾树，树枯，下有蛇，长七八丈，县而死。御览九百三十四 The "寿光侯" was a man from the "汉章帝" era, known for exorcising hundreds of ghosts and evil spirits. A woman was afflicted by an evil spirit, and the lord exorcised it, revealing a large snake. There was also a tree where anyone who stopped under it would die; the lord exorcised the tree, causing it to wither, and a snake about 7-8 meters long fell dead from it. *Yulian* (Imperial Review) 934.

In Han Zhong there was a ghostly deity known as Luan Hou, who often dwelled on the ceiling and delighted in eating preserved fish. As cited in *Shu Chao*, volume 146, it is said that the Han River deity resided on the ceiling, enjoyed eating preserved vegetables, and could foretell auspicious or inauspicious events. During the Ganlu era, a great locust plague arose; wherever the locusts passed, the crops were entirely destroyed. The governor sent an envoy to inform Luan Hou, offering preserved fish as a sacrifice. The deity said to the official, "Locusts are a minor matter; I shall deal with them at once." After he spoke, the locusts suddenly flew out. The official vaguely recalled that the creatures resembled crows and made a sound like that of a waterfowl. When the official returned, he gave a full report to the governor. Indeed, a countless number of birds came to feast on the locusts, and in a short time, they were all devoured. *Guangji*, volume 292. Xianyu Ji of Xi He served as the governor of Qinghe during the Jianwu era. He claimed to have spent six million coins to build a house, but before it was completed, he died. Zhao Gao took his place, and upon calculating the costs, found that only two million coins had been spent. Wu Guan Huang Bing and Gongcao Liu Shang claimed that the excess funds had been taken by Ji himself, and so they submitted a petition to confiscate Ji’s land, property, and servants, sending his wife and children to Ri Nan. Soon after, the ghost of Ji entered the government office during the day and, together with Shang and Bing, reviewed the accounts, determining that the remaining two million coins had been hidden by Shang and his associates. Ji then submitted a petition to the emperor, requesting that the matter be referred to Shang, and the emperor ordered the return of Ji’s land and property. *Yulian*, volume 836. Lord Shouguang, a man of the Eastern Han during the reign of Emperor Zhang, was able to summon and subdue a hundred ghosts and spirits. There was a woman who had been afflicted by a spirit, and Lord Shouguang summoned the spirit and captured a large snake. There was also a great tree, and anyone who stopped at it would die. Lord Shouguang summoned the tree, and it withered; beneath it was a snake, seven or eight zhang long, which was hung and died. *Yulian*, volume 934.

A woman named Su E from Guangxin, Cangwu, was traveling and staying at the Goose-Running Pavilion in Gao'an when she was killed by the pavilion keeper Gong Shou. Her maid, Zengfu, was also killed, and they took her belongings, burying them under the pavilion. The Inspector of Jiaozhi, Zhou Chang, stayed at the pavilion during his inspection and discovered Gong Shou's criminal acts. He reported it and had him executed. This is recorded in the *Wen Xuan* by Jiang Yan, cited in the *Yuelan* volume 194, fully documented in the *Shu Lin* volume 74, which cites the *Yuan Hun Zhi*. It notes that "Gao'an" should be "Gao Yao" and "Zhou Chang" should be "He Chang," indicating a correction in the text. Bao Xuan, a former Inspector of the Capital Commandery from Shangdang, had two sons named Zi Du. In his youth, he was appointed as an official in charge of financial matters. On the road, he met a scholar who was traveling alone and suddenly suffered from a heart attack. Zi Du got off his carriage to massage him, and he died suddenly. He did not know the man's name, but found a scroll of white paper and ten pieces of silver. He sold one piece of silver to bury the man, placed the rest under his head, and put the scroll on his abdomen. He cried and said, "If your spirit is aware, I will let your family know where you are. Now I have a mission to fulfill, so I cannot stay long." And then he left. When he arrived in the capital, a fine horse followed him. No one could get close to it, except for Zi Du. Zi Du returned, and lost his way. He met a family of a marquis inside the pass and stayed overnight. He saw the master and called the servant to deliver a message. The servant came out to see the horse and went inside to report to the marquis, saying, "The guest from outside is stealing the lost fine horse."

The Duke said, "Bao Zidu is a high-ranking scholar from Shangdang; he must have something to say." The Duke asked, "How did you obtain this horse? According to the *Lüyi*, it was said that this was my horse, which I lost without reason years ago." Bao Zidu replied, "During the last census, I met a scholar who died on the road." He then detailed the story. The Duke was shocked and exclaimed, "This is my son!" The Duke welcomed the funeral procession, opened the coffin, and saw the silver book as described. The Duke then took his entire family to the imperial court to recommend Bao Zidu, and Bao Zidu's reputation became prominent. According to the *Wenyuan Yinghua*, a variant version of the text omits four characters and lists different titles for Bao Zidu, such as serving as a high-ranking official in the capital, a governor of Yuzhou, and a Sili Xiaowei. Bao Zidu's descendants, Zi Yong and Sun Yu, also held the position of Sili Xiaowei. When they became high officials, they all rode gray horses. Therefore, a folk song in the capital went: "The gray horses of the Bao family, thrice entered the Sili Xiaowei and twice entered the high official ranks. Though the horses are weary, they still walk gracefully." As recorded in the *Lüyi* and *Wenyuan Yinghua*.

There was an evil spirit in Ruyan that often wore the attire of a governor, went to the government office gate, and beat the drum, causing great distress to the entire county. When Fei Changfang realized it was a spirit, he scolded it. The spirit immediately removed its clothes, knelt down, and begged to be transformed into a turtle, as large as a cart wheel. Fei Changfang then wrote a document in the style of a governor, ordered the Lord of Gebi, and knelt down with tears in his eyes, taking the document away. When he saw it, the document was placed at the edge of the pond, and the turtle's neck was wrapped around it, causing it to die. (Recorded in the *Yingyuan Zhi*, volume 468) Fei Changfang could control spirits. Later, the Lord of Donghai met the Lord of Gebi and had relations with his wife. As a result, Fei Changfang ordered the Lord of Gebi to be imprisoned for three years, and Donghai suffered a severe drought. When Fei Changfang arrived in Donghai and saw the people praying for rain, he ordered the Lord of Gebi to release the rain, and it rained heavily. (Recorded in the *Yingyuan Zhi*, volume 393) Fei Changfang could also shrink the earth's veins. While sitting at home, he went to the market to buy fish paste. In one day, people saw him several times from a thousand miles away. (Recorded in the *Yingyuan Zhi*, volume 862) Empress Feng of Emperor Huan of Han died of illness. During the reign of Emperor Ling, thieves dug up her tomb. Seventy years later, her appearance remained unchanged. She was only slightly cold, and they engaged in sexual relations, leading to fights and killings. The family of Empress Dowager Dou was executed, and they wanted to have Empress Feng accompany the ancestors in Xiangpi. Chen Gongda argued that although she was once favored, her body was defiled, and she should not accompany the supreme ancestor. So they had Empress Dowager Dou accompany the ancestors instead. (Recorded in the *Yingyuan Zhi*, volume 35)

Jiang Ziwén, during the late Han Dynasty, served as the magistrate of Mòlíng. He believed that his bones were blue, and he would become a deity after his death. *Taiping Yulan* Volume 375 Húwú Bān, carrying a letter for Tàishān Fǔjūn, requested the River God to give him blue silk shoes, which were very exquisite. *Taiping Yulan* Volume 697 During the time of Yuán Běnchū, a deity appeared in the eastern part of the river and was known as Dùsuǒ Jūn. People built a temple for him. A woman from the Sū family in Yǎnzhōu, who was ill, went to pray. She saw a man dressed in white cloth, wearing a single robe and a tall hat. The hat resembled a fish head. He said to Dùsuǒ Jūn, "We once ate white plums together at the foot of Lín Lú Mountain, and it has been only three thousand years since then. Time passes quickly, making one feel melancholic!" After he left, Dùsuǒ Jūn said, "This is the South Sea Lord." *Qí Mín Yào Shù* Volume 10, *Chuxue Ji* Volume 28, *Leiji* Volume 86, *Taiping Yulan* Volumes 882 and 968, *Leilin Zasay* Volume 15 Huá Xīn (or *Zǐ Yú* as cited in *Taiping Yulan*), when he was a student, once stayed outside someone's door. The owner's wife gave birth at night, and soon after, two officials came to the door, stepping back and saying to each other, "The public official is here!" They hesitated for a long time. One official said, "The register is set, how can we allow him to stay!" Then they led Huá Xīn to bow, and he was taken inside. When they came out and walked together, one official said, "How many years will he live?" The other said, "Three years." When it was morning, Huá Xīn left. Later, he wanted to verify this, so he went to ask about the child when it reached three years old, and indeed, the child had died. Huá Xīn then realized that he would become a public official, and he was delighted, saying, "I knew I would become a public official, and indeed, I later became the Grand Marshal." *Wei Zhi* *Hua Xīn Zhuan* *Taiping Yulan* Volumes 361 and 467, with the final sentence cited.

Jiang Ji was a commander. One day, his wife had a dream in which her deceased son wept and said, "Life and death are different paths! In life, I was the son of an official, but now in the underworld, I am a low-ranking official at Mount Tai. I am weary, oppressed, and humiliated, and there is no way to describe my suffering. Today, the imperial ancestral temple has summoned a singer named Sun A to be the new underworld official. I hope you will plead with my lord to request Sun A, so that I can be moved to a better place." After he finished speaking, his mother suddenly woke up. The next day, she told Jiang Ji about the dream. Jiang Ji said, "It was just a dream, nothing to be surprised about." That night, she dreamed again, and the son said, "I came to welcome the new official, who is waiting at the imperial ancestral temple. Before he leaves, I will come back to say goodbye. The new official will depart at noon tomorrow, and there will be many preparations before his departure, so I will not be able to return. Farewell forever from here. My lord is stubborn and difficult to convince, so I am pleading with you again. Why not just try it once?" He then described the new official's appearance in great detail. The next day, Jiang Ji's wife pleaded with her husband again, saying, "I dreamed this again last night. Although it was just a dream, it feels too real. Why not just try it once?" Jiang Ji then sent someone to investigate at the imperial ancestral temple and found Sun A. The descriptions of his appearance matched exactly what the son had said. Jiang Ji wept and said, "I have wronged my son!" He then met with Sun A and told him everything. Sun A was not afraid of dying but was happy to become an underworld official, and he only wished that Jiang Ji would believe him.

The father said, "If it is as you say, then it is as my husband wishes. I do not know what position your honorable son desires." The son replied, "Give him whatever he likes in the afterlife." The father said, "I will follow your instructions!" He then heavily rewarded the messenger. After speaking, he sent him back. The father wanted to quickly know the outcome, so he placed a messenger every ten steps from the commander's gate to the temple, to relay the news about Ar. At the hour of Chen (7-9 AM), they reported that Ar was in pain; at the hour of Si (9-11 AM), they reported that Ar was suffering greatly; at noon, they reported that Ar had died. The father wept, saying, "Although I grieve for my son's misfortune, I am also glad that the deceased have consciousness." After a month or so, the son returned and told his mother, "He has already been promoted to the position of Lushi (a minor official)." According to the *Records of the Three Kingdoms* and the *Continued Biographies of Eminent Women*, the *Annotated Collection of Various Sayings* records that Jie Ji (Peng Zhen) was from the state of Chu, and his son died ten years ago. His wife dreamed that her deceased son told her, "I am now in the underworld and am serving as the recorder for Mount Tai. I wish to avoid being under the authority of Sun A and be placed in a place of happiness." His mother was shocked and woke up, crying and telling Jie Ji. Jie Ji was a strong and resolute man and initially did not believe her. However, that night, she dreamed again that her son returned as before and told her the same thing, and she told Jie Ji again. Jie Ji summoned Sun A and told him about the dream. Sun A said, "Yes, I will take care of it." After a few days, Sun A fell ill and died. A few days later, his wife dreamed that her deceased son came to her and said, "I am now in the underworld and have been appointed as the recorder for Mount Tai, thanks to Sun A's efforts." This story is recorded in the *Strange Tales* and other texts, but the details may vary slightly due to differences in transmission and interpretation.

The *Wu Xuancao Lingshi* (*Selections of the Wu State's Officials*) records that Liu Zhuo, an official in Changsha, fell into a state of delirium and dreamed of a person who gave him a white silk robe, saying, "When your robe is soiled, burn it to cleanse it." When Liu Zhuo awoke, he indeed found the robe by his side. Whenever the robe was soiled, he would cleanse it by fire. (*Chu Xue Ji*, Volume 26; *Yuelan*, Volume 399) During the Wu period, Deng Zhuo of Changsha was said to be a deity. He was sent a horse to fetch him, but upon arriving, he saw something below that was fluttering like snow. Deng Zhuo asked the horseman, "Is this the white cranes flying over the sea?" A person then took several young cranes and gave them to Deng Zhuo. (*Dunhuang Shishi Suo Chu Tangren Xieben Leishu Canjuan*) The *Da Sima* (Grand Marshal) Tang Rui of Hebei, whose name is also recorded as Tang Ling or Tang Yang, fell ill with malaria as a young man and fled to a shrine. Someone called out, "Du Di, Du Di," and Tang Rui responded, "Yes!" He then went to the entrance of the shrine, where a person said, "Take this book." He received a scroll of white paper, which contained a method for exorcising and punishing ghosts. (*Shu Chao*, Volume 87; *Yuelan*, Volumes 532 and 743)

In the *Imperial View*, under the entry "Changsha," it is recorded that Liu Zhuo, a clerk in the Office of Selections in the State of Wu, fell into a delirious state and dreamed of a man who gave him a single white robe made of Yue silk, saying: "If your robe becomes stained, simply burn it and it will become clean." When Liu awoke, he indeed found the robe by his side. Whenever it became soiled, he would burn it to cleanse it. *Notes on Early Learning*, vol. 26; *Imperial View*, vol. 399. During the time of the State of Wu, Deng Zhuo of Changsha became a deity. He was sent for by Ma Qiong, who was suspected to be the one welcoming him. Below them, objects were seen falling like snow. Deng asked the man holding the horse, who said: "These are white cranes flying from the sea." A man then gave Deng several young cranes. This account comes from a Tang dynasty manuscript fragment of a compendium discovered in the Dunhuang Caves. Grand General Tang Rui of He Nei, known as Shengqing, suffered from a feverish illness in his youth and fled to a shrine of the gods. Someone called out, "Du Di!" Shengqing responded, "Yes!" He rose to the gate, and the person said, "Take this book with you." He obtained a single scroll of plain paper, which contained a method for summoning and reprimanding a hundred ghosts. The exorcisms were effective. *Book Selections*, vol. 87; *Imperial View*, vol. 532 and 743, where it is stated that he was then cured.

In the *Yuelu*, there is a story about Zhang Fen from Wei Commandery. Zhang Fen was a very wealthy man, but he suddenly fell into poverty. He then sold his house to the Liyang Cheng family. After the Cheng family moved in, they suffered from a series of misfortunes, including deaths and illnesses. Zhang Fen then resold the house to He Wen, a person from the Jing region, according to the *Yuelu*. He Wen then took a knife and climbed onto the beam in the northern hall of the house. At the second watch of the night, he suddenly saw a man who was more than ten feet tall, wearing a tall hat and yellow clothing. The man ascended the hall and called out, asking, "Why is there a living human smell in the small waist house?" He Wen replied, "There is none." Shortly after, another man appeared, wearing a tall hat and blue clothing, followed by yet another man wearing a tall hat and white clothing. The questions and answers were the same as before. Just before dawn, He Wen descended to the hall and called out to them as before. He asked, "Who is the one wearing yellow clothing?" The man replied, "It is gold! It is under the west wall of the hall." He then asked, "Who is the one wearing blue clothing?" The man replied, "It is money! It is five steps from the well in front of the hall." He then asked, "Who is the one wearing white clothing?" The man replied, "It is silver! It is under the northeast corner pillar of the wall." Finally, he asked, "Who are you?" The man replied, "I am the pestle! I am under the stove." At dawn, He Wen dug according to the directions given, and he found five hundred pounds of gold and five hundred pounds of silver, as well as over ten million coins. He then took the pestle and burned it, and the house became peaceful and safe. This story is recorded in the *Guangji* (卷四百) and the *Yuelu* (卷七百六十二). --- In the *Shuolin* (珠林), there is a story about a young man named Song Dingbo from Nanyang. When he was young, he encountered a ghost while traveling at night. The ghost asked, "Who are you?" Song Dingbo replied, "I am a ghost too." The ghost then asked, "Where do you want to go?" Song Dingbo replied, "I want to go to the market in Wuan." The ghost said, "I will accompany you."

Ding Bo also wanted to go to the market in Wan. They walked together for several miles. The ghost said, "Walking is very tiring; let's take turns carrying each other." Ding Bo said, "Very good." The ghost then carried Ding Bo for several miles. The ghost said, "You are very heavy! Are you not a ghost?" Ding Bo replied, "I just died, so I am heavy." Ding Bo then carried the ghost, and the ghost was almost weightless. They took turns carrying each other several times. Ding Bo said again, "I just died and don't know what ghosts are afraid of." The ghost said, "We only dislike being spat on." They walked along the road and came across water. Ding Bo told the ghost to cross first; it crossed silently. When Ding Bo crossed, he made a splash. The ghost said, "Why did you make a noise?" Ding Bo said, "I just died and am not used to crossing water. Don't be surprised!" As they approached the market in Wan, Ding Bo placed the ghost on his head and held it tightly. The ghost shouted loudly, "Let me down!" but Ding Bo did not listen. He went straight to the market in Wan, and when the ghost touched the ground, it turned into a sheep. Ding Bo sold the sheep, afraid it would turn back, so he spat on it. He got 1,500 coins and then left. At the time, people said, "Ding Bo sold a ghost and got 1,500 coins."

A Daughter of the Northern Land's Minister Fu, once crafted a mouse from reeds and placed it on the ground with cunning. The mouse suddenly began to move and entered the ground at the threshold of the house. She then made another mouse from reeds and cast a spell over it, saying, "If you become a household spirit, you should move again; otherwise, you will not move." When placed on the ground, the mouse moved again as before. They immediately dug within the boundary to search, but the mouse had entered the ground several feet, and nothing was found. After this, all the daughters died one after another. This story is recorded in the *Yuehui* (《夜怪录》), volume 360. Once upon a time, in the county of Anle under the commandery of Fanyang, there was a man surnamed Peng whose family had made a living from hunting and shooting. His son followed his father into the mountains, where the father suddenly fell to the ground and transformed into a white deer. The son cried out in sorrow and tried to chase after the deer, but it leapt away and disappeared into the distance, leaving no trace. From that day on, the son never picked up a bow again. It was not until his grandson that the family resumed learning archery. One day, the grandson suddenly shot a white deer and found a Taoist seven-star talisman hidden between the deer's antlers, along with the clear markings of the ancestor's name, surname, and date of birth. Upon seeing this, he felt a deep sense of regret. He then burned his bow and arrows. This story is recorded in the *Wuyin Yulan* (《御览》), volume 888.

A Daoist practitioner in Beihai, a region in ancient China, possessed the ability to enable living people to see the dead. A woman from the same region had passed away several years prior, and her family, upon hearing of the Daoist's powers, sought him out. They requested, "We wish to see the deceased one more time, and we would not regret it, as recorded in the *Yuzhan*." The Daoist instructed them on how to proceed, and they were able to see the woman. They conversed with her, expressing both sorrow and joy, and the emotional bond they shared was as strong as it had been in life. After a long period of time, they heard the sound of a drum, which caused them great distress. Unable to leave the house, they slammed the door shut and fled. In their haste, the hem of their clothing was caught in the door, tearing off as they escaped. A year later, the person who had seen the woman died. When they were buried, upon opening the coffin, they found the hem of the woman's clothing beneath the lid. This account is cited in the *Wenxuan* by Jiang Yan and referenced in the *Yuzhan* (Volume 884). In Chenliu, there was a man named Shi Jun, courtesy name Weiming. He once fell ill and, on the brink of death, told his mother, "If I am to be resurrected, bury me and place a staff upright on my grave; if the staff is pulled out, I will rise again." After his death, he was buried as instructed, with a staff placed on his grave. Seven days later, when they went to check on the grave, they found that the staff had indeed been pulled out. They immediately dug him up, and he returned to life as if nothing had happened. This account is cited in the *Yuzhan* (Volume 710). In Jibei, a man named Xian Chao encountered a celestial maiden who visited him. Her carriage was adorned with five sets of blue and white glassware. This account is cited in the *Yuzhan* (Volume 761) and is recorded in the *Zhu Lin* (Volume 5), which references the *Soushen Ji* (Record of Searching for the Supernatural). A divine figure named Wang Fangping descended to the home of Chen Jiefang and presented him with two swords, one five feet long and the other five feet and three inches long. He named them "Taishan Huan" and told Chen, "These swords will not benefit me, but if you sleep alone, they will protect you from ghosts, and if you go to war, they will prevent harm. Do not use them in the toilet, and do not keep them for too long. After three years, if someone urgently seeks them, give them to them." Indeed, a man named Dai Zhuo offered a million coins to acquire the swords. This account is cited in the *Yuzhan* (Volume 345).

The East Sea God presented a woven blue robe to Chen Jiefang. *Huihui Yulan*, Volume 609. The immortal Ma Gu descended to the home of Cai Jing in Dongyang. Her fingers were four inches long. Cai Jing thought, "This woman indeed has excellent hands; I wish she could scratch my back." Ma Gu became very angry; suddenly, she saw Cai Jing fall to the ground, with blood streaming from his eyes. *Huihui Yulan*, Volume 370. Cai Jing had a spiritual encounter with a deity. When the deity was about to leave, the family saw Cai Jing go to a well to drink water, then mount a horse and depart. Looking at the well, they all saw Cai Jing's skin as if it had shed like a snake's skin, and he never returned. *Huihui Yulan*, Volume 375. Tian Bo served as the governor of Lujiang and moved the capital to deal with evil spirits. If the spirits' lives ended and they did not come to the office within a month, the temple would be destroyed. Only Lu Jun went to see them, introducing himself as a county resident and making an agreement with the governor: within a hundred days, he would be promoted to a major city and wished to be seen. As promised, he later became the governor of Pei, not exceeding the temple, and often saw Lu Jun. After a month, he fell ill and died. *Shuchao*, Volume 76. A woman named Dai Shi from Yuning had been ill for a long time. She saw a small stone and said, "You have a spirit; if you can cure my illness, I will serve you." That night, she dreamed that someone told her, "I will protect you." Later, she gradually recovered and established a temple for the stone, naming it the Stone Lord Temple. *Huihui Yulan*, Volume 51.

A man named Tan, who was forty years old and unmarried, was deeply moved by reading the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry). One midnight, a girl around fifteen or sixteen years old, with unparalleled beauty and attire, came to him and said they would be husband and wife. She warned him, "I am different from others, do not use fire to see me. Only after three years can you see me." They became husband and wife and had a son who was already two years old. Unable to resist, Tan waited until she was asleep at night and secretly looked at her with a light. He saw that above her waist, she had flesh like a person, but below her waist, there were only dry bones. When she woke up, she said, "You have wronged me, I am almost alive. Why couldn't you wait one more year and see me?" Tan apologized and wept uncontrollably, saying, "Though we must part forever, yet I think of our child. If he is poor and cannot live on his own, come with me for a while, and I will leave something for you." Tan followed her and entered a magnificent hall with extraordinary rooms and furnishings. She gave him a pearl robe and said, "It will sustain you." She then took a piece of his clothes, left it behind, and departed. Later, Tan took the robe to the Wang family in Suizhou and sold it, receiving a million coins. The king recognized it and said, "This was my daughter's robe, this must be from a grave." He then examined it, and Tan told the truth. The king still did not believe, so he went to see the tomb, which was intact. When he opened it, he indeed found a piece of clothing under the coffin lid. He called the boy, who looked exactly like the king's daughter, and the king then believed him. The king immediately summoned Tan, gave him another gift, and made him a son-in-law. He proclaimed his son as a court official. This story is recorded in the *Guangji* (Extensive Records) volume 316.

Linzhi Cai Zhi was a county official. One day, while delivering a letter to the governor, he suddenly lost his way and reached the foot of Mount Tai. He saw something like a city and entered to deliver the letter. Inside, he saw an official with a very strict retinue, all resembling a governor. The official was given a letter and said, "You will deliver this letter to my grandson." The official replied, "Your Excellency, who is your grandson?" The other official replied, "I am the god of Mount Tai, and my grandson is the Heavenly Emperor." The official was startled and realized he had arrived in a non-human realm. He then left the place, riding a horse. After a while, he suddenly arrived at the Taiwei Palace of the Heavenly Emperor. The attendants and ministers were all like a king. After delivering the letter, the emperor ordered him to sit and offered him food and drink. He then asked, "How many family members do you have?" Cai Zhi replied, "My parents, wife, and I are all deceased, and I have not remarried." The emperor asked, "How many years has it been since your wife died?" Cai Zhi replied, "Three years." The emperor asked, "Do you want to see her?" Cai Zhi replied, "Thank you, Your Majesty!" The emperor immediately ordered the Minister of the Household Department to instruct the God of Life to remove Cai Zhi's wife from the death register and add her to the birth register, then ordered her to accompany Cai Zhi back. Cai Zhi then woke up and returned home, dug up his wife's grave, and saw that her body had indeed come back to life. In a short while, she sat up and spoke as if nothing had happened. This story is recorded in the *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records) volume 375.

Ding Bozhao of Liaodong recounted that his family once had a guest named Cijie, who, after his death, was grateful for the kindness shown to him and would often bring strange and wonderful objects to the family. Once, during the winter solstice, he requested some melons, and beautiful melons appeared before them, though no form was visible. *Wuyin yulan* 978 Liu Boyi, the northern postal inspector of Runan, had great talent and strategy. He was traveling and stayed at Jiwu Pavilion for the night. Someone warned him, “This pavilion is not suitable for lodging.” Liu Boyi decided to stay alone, extinguished the fire, and recited the Five Classics before lying down to sleep. Soon, he turned his head to the east, tied his feet with a cotton scarf, and covered them with a headband. He drew his sword and loosened his belt. At night, an unusual creature approached gradually and suddenly covered Liu Boyi. Liu Boyi bent up, covered it with his sleeve, tied it with a belt, and called for fire to illuminate it. He saw an old, red, hairless fox. He held it down with the fire and killed it. The next day, he inspected the upper floors and rooms and found hundreds of human hairs, evidence of the fox's past victims. Thus, the pavilion became peaceful and quiet. An old saying goes, “A fox must have shaved the heads of a thousand people to become a god.” *Wuyin yulan* 253 Jiang Yan, while at Qingquan Mountain in Fuchun County, saw a beautiful woman from afar, dressed in purple and singing. Jiang Yan approached her, and after walking dozens of steps, the woman disappeared, leaving only the stone she was standing on. This happened several times, and eventually, he found a purple jade stone, one foot wide. Another time, Bing Lang, at Jiutian Mountain, saw a bird that looked like a chicken, was red in color, and made a sound like a flute. When he shot it, it immediately entered a hole. Bing Lang then dug into the stone and found a red jade stone in the shape of the bird. *Guangji* 401

In Pengcheng, there was a man who married a woman, but he was not pleased with her and stayed out overnight. After over a month, the woman asked, "Why haven't you come back?" The man replied, "You always go out at night, so I haven't come back." The woman said, "I never go out at night." The husband was surprised and the woman said, "You must have some ulterior motives; you must be bewitched by someone else! If anyone else comes, you should hold onto them; use a light to see what it is." Later, the person she wanted to bring back acted as if she was hesitating to enter, and someone pushed her forward from behind. Once on the bed, the husband grabbed her and said, "Why do you go out every night?" The woman said, "You have been going back and forth with the woman next door, and now you are trying to cover it up by pretending to be possessed by ghosts!" The husband let her go and lay down with her. In the middle of the night, he realized, "The demon has bewitched me, not my wife." He then reached out to grab it and shouted for a light. It gradually shrank, and when he looked at it, he found a carp, two feet long. (From the "Miscellaneous Records," volume 469) During the reign of Emperor Jing, a county official named Wang Chen from Chengyang County once made a pillow and slept on it. After a while, he heard someone calling from below the stove, "Why are you under someone's head?" He replied, "I see the pillow, but I can't move. Come to me." He came and drank from a jar. (From the "Imperial Reader," volumes 701 and 760)

During the Zhengshi era, Prince Zhou Nan of Zhongshan served as the magistrate of Xiangyi County. One day, a mouse wearing human-like clothing emerged from a hole in the hall. It spoke, saying, "Prince Zhou, you will die on the day of the month." Zhou Nan did not respond, and the mouse returned to its hole. Later, on the predicted day, the mouse reappeared, dressed in a hat and red robe, and said, "Prince Zhou, you will die at noon." Again, Zhou Nan did not respond, and the mouse slowly retreated into the hole. Soon after, it emerged again and said, "The sun is about to reach noon." The mouse entered and exited the hole several times, repeating its message. When noon arrived, the mouse said, "Prince Zhou, since you do not respond, what more can I say?" It then fell over and died, and its clothing disappeared. Zhou Nan sent someone to inspect the mouse, and it was found to be exactly like a regular mouse. (Source: *Shu Chao* 158, *Lei Ju* 95, *Tai Ping Guang Ji* 885, 911) On the northern hill of Xin County in Wuchang, there is a stone called the "Waiting Wife Stone," which looks like a person standing. According to legend, once there was a virtuous woman whose husband went far to serve the state in times of crisis. The woman, accompanied by her young child, farewelled him at this mountain and, while standing and waiting, transformed into a stone. (Source: *Tai Ping Guang Ji* 888) Around Mount Lu, there are often thousands of wild geese that gather in flocks. The elders tell a story that once a cat ate one of the geese, and the next day, the cat called out on the sandbar, as if it were tied up. (Source: *Tai Ping Guang Ji* 919) Laozi traveled west, and the gatekeeper Yin Xi of the Hangu Pass saw purple qi (a sign of great fortune) floating over the pass. Laozi indeed rode an ox and passed through. (Source: *Shi Ji: Laozi, Zhuangzi, Shen, and Han Biographies* Index)

《列异传》 三国魏 曹丕 黄帝葬桥山，山崩无尸，惟剑舄存。御览六百九十七 秦穆公时，陈仓人掘地得异物；其形不类狗，亦不似羊，众莫能名。三句御览引有一作若羊非羊若猪非猪牵以献穆公，道逢二童子。童子曰：“此名为媪，御览作蝹述注云蝹音袄常在地下食死人脑。若欲杀之，以柏插其头。”已上亦见御览三百七十五又九百五十四媪复曰：“彼二童子，名为陈宝；得雄者王，得雌者霸。”陈仓人舍媪逐二童子，童子化为雉，飞入平林。陈仓人告穆公，穆公发徒大猎，果得其雌。又化为石，置之汧渭之间。至文公，为立祠，名陈宝。雄飞南集，今南阳雉县其地也。已上广记四百六十一亦引秦欲表其符，故以名县。每陈仓祠时，有赤光长十余丈，从雉县来，入陈宝祠中，有声如雄鸡。类聚九十御览九百十七书钞八十九引宋五句！

史记封禅书索○引列异传云：陈仓人得异物，以献之。道遇二童子云：“此名为媦，在地下食死人脑。”媦乃言：“彼二童子，名陈宝；得雄者王，得雌者霸。”乃逐童子，化为雉。秦穆公大猎，果获其雌，为立祠。祭则有光雷电之声。雄止南阳，有赤光长十余丈，来入陈仓祠中。 武都故道县有怒特祠，云神本南山大梓也，昔秦文公二十七年伐之，树疮，随合。秦文公乃遣四十人持斧斫之，犹不断。疲士一人，伤足不能去，卧树下。闻鬼相与言曰：“劳攻战乎？”其一曰：“足为劳矣。”又曰：“秦公必持不休。”答曰：“其如我何？”又曰：赤灰跋于子何如？”乃默无言。卧者以告，令士皆赤衣，随所斫，以灰跋树，断化为牛入水。故秦为立祠。水经渭水篇注

秦文公时，梓树化为牛，以骑击之，骑不胜；或堕地，髻解被发，牛畏之入水。故秦因是置旄头骑，使先驱。后汉书光武纪注书钞一百三十御览三百四十一类聚九十四引作牛畏之没丰水中秦乃立怒特祠 干将莫邪为楚王作剑，三年而成。剑有雄雌，天下名器也，乃以雌剑献君，藏其雄者。谓其妻曰：“吾藏剑在南山之阴，北山之阳；松生石上，剑在其中矣。君若觉杀我，尔生男，以告之。”及至君觉，杀干将。妻后生男，名赤鼻，告之。赤鼻斫南山之松，不得剑；忽于屋柱中得之。楚王梦一人，眉广三寸，辞欲报仇。购求甚急，乃逃朱兴山中。遇客，欲为之报；乃刎首，将以奉楚王。客令镬煮之，头三日三夜跳不烂。王往观之，客以雄剑倚拟王，王头堕镬中；客又自刎。三头悉烂，不可分别，分葬之，名曰三王冢。御览三百四十三

魏公子无忌曾在室中读书之际，有一鸠飞入案下，鹞逐而杀之。忌忿其搏击，因令国内捕鹞，遂得二百余头。忌按剑至笼曰：“昨搦鸠者当低头服罪，不是者可奋翼。”有一鹞俯伏不动。广记四百六十 鲁少千者，得仙人符。楚王少女为魅所病，请少千。少千未至数十里止宿，夜有乘鳖盖车从数千骑来，自称伯敬，候少千。遂请内酒数○，肴餤数案。临别言：“楚王女病，是吾所为。君若相为一还，我谢君二十万。”千受钱，即为还，从他道诣楚，为治之。于女舍前，有排户者，但闻云：“少千欺汝翁！”遂有风声西北去，视处有血满盆。女遂绝气，夜半乃苏。王使人寻风，于城西北得一死蛇，长数丈，小蛇千百，伏死其旁。后诏下郡县，以其日月，大司农失钱二十万，太官失案数具；少千载钱上书，具陈说，天子异之。广记四百五十六

任城公孙达，甘露中为陈郡，卒官，将敛，儿及郡吏数十人临丧。达有五岁儿，忽作灵语，音声如父，呵众人：“哭已上十二字御览引阙止！吾欲有所道。”因呼诸儿，以次教戒。儿悲哀不能自胜，乃慰之曰：“四时之运，犹有所终；人物短脆，当无穷。”如此数千语，皆成文章。儿乃问曰：“人死皆无知，惟大人聪明殊特，独有神耶？”答曰：“存亡之事，未易可言；鬼神之事，非人知也。”索纸作言，辞义满纸。投地云：“封书与魏君宰，?有信来，即以付之。”其?君宰果有信来。御览八百八十四广记三百十六

汉中有鬼神栾侯，常在承尘上喜食鲊菜，书钞一百四十六引云汉川神常在承尘上喜食?菜能知吉凶。甘露中，大蝗起：所经处禾稼辄尽。太守遣使告栾侯，祀以鲊菜。侯谓吏曰：“蝗虫小事，辄当除之。”。言讫，翕然飞出。吏仿彿其状类鸠，声如水鸟。吏还，具白太守？果有众鸟亿万，来食蝗虫，须臾皆尽。广记二百九十二 西河鲜于冀，建武中为清河太守，言出钱六百万作屋，未成而死。赵高代之，计功用钱，凡二百万耳。五官黄秉功曹刘商言是冀所自取，便表没冀田宅奴婢，妻子送日南。俄而白日冀鬼入府，与商秉等共计较，定余钱二百万，皆商等匿。冀乃表自列付商上，诏还冀田宅。御览八百三十六 寿光侯者，汉章帝时人，劾百鬼众魅。有妇为魅所疾，侯劾得大蛇；又有大树，人止之者死，侯劾树，树枯，下有蛇，长七八丈，县而死。御览九百三十四

汉中有鬼神栾侯，常在承尘上喜食鲊菜，书钞一百四十六引云汉川神常在承尘上喜食?菜能知吉凶。甘露中，大蝗起：所经处禾稼辄尽。太守遣使告栾侯，祀以鲊菜。侯谓吏曰：“蝗虫小事，辄当除之。”。言讫，翕然飞出。吏仿彿其状类鸠，声如水鸟。吏还，具白太守？果有众鸟亿万，来食蝗虫，须臾皆尽。广记二百九十二 西河鲜于冀，建武中为清河太守，言出钱六百万作屋，未成而死。赵高代之，计功用钱，凡二百万耳。五官黄秉功曹刘商言是冀所自取，便表没冀田宅奴婢，妻子送日南。俄而白日冀鬼入府，与商秉等共计较，定余钱二百万，皆商等匿。冀乃表自列付商上，诏还冀田宅。御览八百三十六 寿光侯者，汉章帝时人，劾百鬼众魅。有妇为魅所疾，侯劾得大蛇；又有大树，人止之者死，侯劾树，树枯，下有蛇，长七八丈，县而死。御览九百三十四

苍梧广信女子苏娥，行宿高安鹄奔亭，为亭长龚寿所杀，及婢致富，取其财物，埋致楼下。交阯刺史周敞行部宿亭，觉寿奸罪，奏之，杀寿。文选江淹诣建平王上书注御览一百九十四 具见洙林七十四引冤魂志高安作高要周敞作何敞当据正 故司隶校尉上党鲍宣字二字事类赋注引有子都，少时举上计掾；于道中遇一书生，独行无伴，卒得心痛，子都下车为按摩，奄忽而卒。不知姓字，有素书一卷，银十饼。御览一引作银饼二即卖一饼以殡殓，其余银以枕之，素书着腹上。哭之，谓曰：“若子灵魂有知，当令子家知子在此。今奉使命，不获久留。”遂辞而去。至京师，有骏马随之；人莫能得近，唯子都得近。子都归，行失道；遇一关内侯家，日?住宿，见主人，呼奴通刺。奴出见马，入白侯曰：“外客盗骑昔所失骏马。”。

侯曰：“鲍子都上党高士，必应有语。”侯问曰：“君何以致此马？类聚引作若此乃吾马昔年无故失之。”子都曰：“昔年上计，遇一书生，卒死道中。”具述其事。侯乃惊愕曰：“此吾儿也！”侯迎丧开椁视银书如所言。侯乃举家诣阙上荐，子都声名遂显。御览一引无此四字作辟公府侍御史豫州牧司隶校尉，至子永孙昱，并为司隶。及其为公，皆乘骢马。故京师歌曰：“鲍氏骢，三入司隶再入公。马虽疲，行步工。类聚八十三御览二百五十又八百十二又八百九十一又八百九十七书钞六十一引子永已下工作通！！

汝南有妖，常作太守服，诣府门椎鼓，郡患之。及费长房知是魅，乃呵之。即解衣冠叩头，乞自改变为老鳖；大如车轮。长房复就太守服作一札，敕葛陂君；叩头流涕持札去。视之，以札立陂边，以颈绕之而死。广记四百六十八 费长房能使神，后东海君见葛陂君，淫其夫人；于是长房敕系三年，而东海大旱。长房至东海，见其请雨；乃敕葛陂君出之，即大雨。御览八百八十二广记三百九十三 费长房又能缩地脉，坐客在家，至市买鲊；一日之间，人见之千里之外者数次。类聚七十二御览八百六十二 汉桓帝冯夫人病亡。灵帝时，有贼盗发冢，七十余年，颜色如故；但小冷，共奸通，至斗争相杀。窦太后家被诛，欲以冯夫人配食下邳；陈公达议：以“贵人虽是先所幸，尸体秽污，不宜配至尊。”乃以窦太后配食。类聚三十五

蒋子文汉末为秣陵尉，自谓骨青，死当为神。御览三百七十五 胡毋班为太山府君赍书，请河伯贻其青丝履，甚精巧也。御览六百九十七 袁本初时有神出河东，号度索君，人共立庙。兖州苏氏母病往祷，见一人，着白布，单衣高冠。冠似鱼头，谓度索君曰：“昔临庐山下共食白李，未久已三千年。日月易得，使人怅然！”去后，度索君曰：“此南海君也。”齐民要术十初学记二十八类聚八十六御览八百八十二又九百六十八 类林杂说十五 华歆御览引作子鱼为诸生时尝宿人门外。主人妇夜产，有顷，两吏诣门，便辟易却，相谓曰：“公在此！”踌躇良久，一吏曰：“籍当定，奈何得住！”乃前歆拜，相将入。出并行，共语曰：“当与几岁？”一人曰：“当三岁。”天明，歆去。后欲验其事，至三岁，故往问儿消息，果已死。歆乃自知当为公，御览引作子鱼喜曰我固当公后果为太尉。魏志华歆传注御览三百六十一又四百六十七引有末句

蒋济为领军，其妻梦见亡儿涕泣曰：“死生异路！我生时为卿相子孙，今在地下为泰山伍伯；憔悴困辱，不可复言。今太庙西讴士孙阿今见召为泰山令，愿母为白侯属阿，令转我得乐处。”言讫，母忽然惊寤。明日以白济，济曰：“梦为尔耳，不足怪也。”明日莫，复梦曰：“我来迎新君，止在庙下；未发之顷，暂得来归。新君明日日中当发，临发多事，不复得归。永辞于此。侯气强难感悟，故自诉于母。愿重启侯，何惜不一试验也。”遂道阿之形状，言甚备悉。天明，母重启侯曰：“昨又梦如此六字依广记引补虽云梦不足怪，此何太适适，亦何惜不一验之。”济乃遣人诣太庙下推问孙阿，果得之；形状证验，悉如儿言？济涕泣曰：“几负吾儿！”于是乃见孙阿，具语其事。阿不惧当死，而喜得为泰山令，惟恐济言不信也。

曰：“若如节下言，阿之愿也。不知贤子欲得何职？”济曰：“随地下乐者与之。”阿曰：“辄当奉教！”乃厚赏之。言讫，遣还，济欲速知其验，从领军门至庙下，十步安一人，以传阿消息。辰时传阿心痛，巳时传阿剧，日中传阿亡。济泣曰：“虽哀吾儿之不幸，且喜亡者有知。”后月余。儿复来，语母曰：“已得转为录事矣。”魏志蒋济传注广记二百七十六 案类林杂说六云蒋济字子通楚郡平阿人也魏文帝时为太尉济有子亡经十年其妻夜梦亡儿告之曰在地下属太山辛苦不可言今领军府南有孙阿者太山府君为录事欲为录事愿毋属孙阿使某得乐处其母惊觉涕泣告济济为人刚强初不信至明夜又梦儿还如前言复告济济召阿至乃述梦中嘱阿阿曰诺如之言地下与君方便经旬日阿病卒后数日其妻还梦见亡儿来曰某地下乃得孙阿太山录事力也魏时人事出列异传文多省略讹夺而与他书所引颇不同

吴选曹令史长沙二字御览引有刘卓病荒，梦见一人，以白越单衫与之，言曰：“汝着衫污，火烧便洁也。”卓觉，果有衫在侧。污辄火浣之。初学记二十六御览三百九十九 吴时长沙邓卓为神，遣马邛疑当作迎之。见物在下，纷纷如雪。卓问持马者，曰：“此海上白鹤飞也。”一人便取鹤子数枚与卓。敦煌石室所出唐人写本类书残卷 大司马河内汤蕤汤御览一引作陵一作阳字圣卿，少时病疟，逃神社中。有人呼杜邸杜邸，圣卿应声曰：“诺！”起至户口，人曰：“取此书去。”得素书一卷，乃谴劾百鬼法也。书钞八十七所劾辄效，御览五百三十二又七百四十三作乃差

吴选曹令史长沙二字御览引有刘卓病荒，梦见一人，以白越单衫与之，言曰：“汝着衫污，火烧便洁也。”卓觉，果有衫在侧。污辄火浣之。初学记二十六御览三百九十九 吴时长沙邓卓为神，遣马邛疑当作迎之。见物在下，纷纷如雪。卓问持马者，曰：“此海上白鹤飞也。”一人便取鹤子数枚与卓。敦煌石室所出唐人写本类书残卷 大司马河内汤蕤汤御览一引作陵一作阳字圣卿，少时病疟，逃神社中。有人呼杜邸杜邸，圣卿应声曰：“诺！”起至户口，人曰：“取此书去。”得素书一卷，乃谴劾百鬼法也。书钞八十七所劾辄效，御览五百三十二又七百四十三作乃差

魏郡二字御览引有张奋者，家巨富。后暴衰，遂卖宅与黎阳程家。程入居，死病相继；转卖与邺人御览引作荆民何文。文日?，乃持刀上北堂中梁上坐。至二更，忽见一人，长丈余，高冠黄衣，升堂呼问：“细腰舍中何以有生人气也？”答曰：“无之。”须臾，有一高冠青衣者，次之，又有高冠白衣者，问答并如前。及将曙，文乃下堂中，如向法呼之。问曰：“黄衣者谁也？”曰：“金也！在堂西壁下。”“青衣者谁也？”曰：“钱也！在堂前井边五步。”“白衣者谁也？”曰：“银也，在墙东北角柱下。”“汝谁也？”曰：“我杵也！在灶下。”及晓，文按次掘之，得金银各五百斤，钱千余万。仍取杵焚之，宅遂清安。广记四百御览七百六十二 南阳宗珠林引作宋定伯，年少时，夜行逢鬼。问曰：“谁？”鬼曰：“鬼也。”鬼曰：“卿复谁？”定伯欺之，言：“我亦鬼也。”鬼问：“欲至何所？”答曰：二句依珠林引补“欲至宛市。鬼言：“

我亦欲至宛市。”共行数里。鬼言：“步行大亟；可共迭相担也。”定伯曰：“大善。”御览广记引并作定伯乃大喜鬼便先担定伯数里。鬼言：“卿大重！将非鬼也？”四字珠林引有定伯言：“我新死，故重耳。”定伯因复担鬼，鬼略无重。如其再三。定伯复言：“我新死，不知鬼悉何所畏忌？”鬼曰：“唯不喜人唾。”于是共道遇水，定伯因命鬼先渡；听之了无声。定伯自渡，漕漼作声。鬼复言：“何以作声？”定伯曰：“新死不习渡水耳。勿怪！”行欲至宛市，定伯便担鬼至头上，急持之，鬼大呼，声咋咋，索下不复听之。径至宛市中，着地化为一羊。便卖之，恐其便化，乃唾之，得钱千五百，乃去。于时言：“定伯卖鬼，得钱千五百。”御览八百八十四又三百八十七珠林六广记三百二十一

北地傅尚书小女，尝拆荻作鼠，以狡狯放地。鼠忽能行，径入户眼土中。又拆荻更作，咒之云：“汝若为家怪者，当更行，不者不动。”放地，便复行如前。即掘限内觅，入地数尺，了无所见。后诸女相继丧亡。广记三百六十 昔番阳郡安乐县有人姓彭，世以捕射为业。儿随父入山，父忽蹶然倒地，乃变成白鹿。儿悲号追，鹿超然远逝，遂失所在。儿于是不捉弓终身。至孙复学射。忽得一白鹿，乃于鹿角间得道家七星符，并有其祖姓名年月分明。视之惋悔。乃烧去弧失。御览八百八十八

北海营陵有道人，能使人与死人相见。同郡人妇死已数年，闻而往见之曰：“愿令我一见死人，御览引作亦不恨。”遂教其见之，于是与妇人相见，言语悲喜，恩情如生。良久时乃闻鼓声悢悢，不能出户，掩门乃走；其裾为户所闭，掣绝而去。后岁余，此人死。家葬之，开见妇棺，盖下有衣裾。文选江淹杂体诗注御览八百八十四 陈留史均字威明，尝得病，临死，谓其母曰：“我得复生，埋我，杖竖我瘗上；若杖拔，出之。”及死，埋杖如其言。七日往视，杖果拔，即掘出之，便平复如故。御览七百十 济北弦超神女来游，车上有壶○青白琉璃五具。御览七百六十一 案此嘉平中事见珠林五引搜神记 有神王方平降陈节方家，以刀二口，一长五尺，一长五尺三寸，名泰山环，语节方曰：“此刀不能为余益，然独卧，可使无鬼，入军不伤；勿以厕溷，且不宜久服。三年后求者急与。”果有戴卓以钱百万请刀。御览三百四十五

东海君以织成青襦遗陈节方。御览六百九十五 神仙麻姑降东阳蔡经家，手爪长四寸，经意曰：“此女子实好佳手，愿得以搔背。”麻姑大怒；忽见经顿地，两目流血。御览三百七十 蔡经与神交，神将去，家人见经诣井上饮水，上马而去。视井上，俱见经皮如蛇蜕，遂不还。御览三百七十五 田伯为庐江太守，移郡淫鬼；命尽到府，一月不自来见，当坏祠。唯庐君往见，自称县民，与府君约，刻百日当迁大都，愿见过。后如期果为沛相公，不过于祠，常见庐君，月余病死。书钞七十六 豫宁女子戴氏久病，出见小石曰：“尔有神，能差我疾者，当事汝。”夜梦人告之：“吾将祐汝。”后渐差，遂为立祠，名石侯祠。御览五十一

谈生者，年四十，无妇。常感激读诗经，夜半有女子可年十五六，姿颜服饰，天下无双，来就生为夫妇之言：“我与人不同，勿以火照我也。三年之后，方可照。”为夫妻，生一儿，已二岁；不能忍，夜伺其寝后，盗照视之，其腰已上生肉如人，腰下但有枯骨。妇觉，遂言曰：“君负我，我垂生矣，何不能忍一岁而竟相照也？”生辞谢，涕泣不可复止，云：“与君虽大义永离，然顾念我儿，若贫不能自偕活者，暂随我去，方遗君物。”生随之去，入华堂，室宇器物不凡。以一珠袍与之，曰：“可以自给。”裂取生衣裾，留之而去。后生持袍诣睢阳王家买之，得钱千万。王识之曰：“是我女袍，此必发墓。”乃取考之，生具以实对。王犹不信，乃视女冢，冢完如故。发视之，果棺盖下得衣裾。呼其儿，正类王女，王乃信之。即召谈生，复赐遗衣，以为主婿。表其儿以为侍中。广记三百十六

临淄蔡支者，为县吏。会奉书谒太守，忽迷路，至岱宗山下。见如城郭，遂入致书。见一官，仪卫甚严，具如太守。乃盛设酒肴毕，付一书，谓曰：“掾为我致此书与外孙也。”吏答曰：“明府外孙为谁？”答曰：“吾太山神也，外孙天帝也。”吏方惊，乃知所至非人间耳。掾出门，乘马所之。有顷，忽达天帝座太微宫殿，左右侍臣俱如天子。支致书讫，帝命坐，赐酒食，仍劳问之曰：“掾家属几人？”对：“父母妻皆已物故，尚未再娶。”帝曰：“君妻卒经几年矣？”支曰：“三年。”帝曰：“君欲见之否？”支曰：恩唯天帝！”帝即命户曹尚书敕司命，辍蔡支妇籍。于生录中，遂命与支相随而去。乃苏归家，因发妻冢，视其形骸，果有生验。须臾起坐，语遂如旧。广记三百七十五

辽东丁伯昭，自说其家有客，字次节，既死，感见待恩，常为本家致奇异物。试腊月中从索瓜，得美瓜数枚来在前，不见形也。御览九百七十八 汝南北部督邮西平刘伯夷有大才略，案行到惧武亭夜宿。或曰：“此亭不可宿。”伯夷乃独住宿，去火诵诗书五经讫卧。有顷，转东首，以絮巾结两足，以帻冠之，拔剑解带。夜时有异物稍稍转近，忽来覆伯夷，伯夷屈起，以袂掩之，以带系魅呼火照之，视得一老狸，色赤无毛，持火烧杀之。明日发视楼屋间，见魅所杀人发数百枚。于是亭遂清静。旧说“狸髡千人，得为神也。御览二百五十三 江严于富春县清泉山，遥见一美女，紫衣而歌，严就之，数十步，女遂隐，唯见所据石。如此数四，乃得一紫玉，广一尺。又邴浪于九田山见鸟，状如鸡，色赤，鸣如吹笙，射之中，即入穴。浪遂凿石，得一赤玉，如鸟形状也。广记四百一

彭城有男子娶妇，不悦之，在外宿。月余日，妇曰：“何故不复入？”男曰：“汝夜辄出，我故不入。”妇曰：“我初不出。”婿惊，妇云：“君自有异志；当为他所惑耳！后有至者，君便抱留之；索火照视之为何物。”后所愿还至，故作其妇前却未入，有一人从后推令前。既上床，婿捉之曰：“夜夜出何为？”妇曰：“君与东舍女往来，而惊欲托鬼魅以前约相掩耳！”婿放之，与共卧。夜半心悟，乃计曰：“魅迷人，非是我妇也。”乃向前揽捉，大呼求火，稍稍缩小，发而视之，得一鲤鱼，长二尺。广记四百六十九 景初中咸一引作城阳县吏王臣夜倦，一引作王巨尝作倦枕枕卧。有顷，闻灶下有呼曰：“文纳何以在人头下？”应曰：“我见枕，不得动，汝来就我。”至乃饮缶一引作饭函也。御览七百一又七百六十

正始中中山王周南为襄邑长，有鼠衣冠从穴中出，在厅事上已上十字依御览引语曰：“周南，尔某月二字御览引有某日当死。”周南不应，鼠还穴。后至期，更冠帻绛衣出，语曰：“周南，汝日中当死。”又不应，鼠缓入穴。须臾，出语曰：“向日适欲中。”鼠入复出，出复入，转更数，语如前语。日适中，鼠曰：“周南，汝不应，我复何道？”言绝，颠蹶而死，即失衣冠。周南使卒四字依御览引补取视之，具如常鼠也。书钞一百五十八类聚九十五御览八百八十五又九百十一 武昌新县北山上有望夫石，状若人立者。传云：昔有贞妇，其夫从役，远赴国难；妇携幼子饯送此山，立望而形化为石。御览八百八十八 庐山左右，常有野鹅数千为群。长老传言：尝有一狸食，明日见狸唤于沙州之上，如见系缚。御览九百十九 老子西游，关令尹喜望见其有紫气浮关，而老子果乘青牛而过。史记老庄申韩列传索隐
